title: Emergency Care: What to Do if Your Cat Collapses and is Unresponsive
meta_desc: A collapsed, unresponsive cat is a critical emergency. Seek immediate veterinary care. Learn what this means and how to help your cat right now.
content_html:
What is a Collapse?

A collapse is a sudden loss of muscle strength that causes a cat to fall and remain unable to get up. When this is accompanied by being unresponsive to touch, it means your cat is likely unconscious or in a state of severe shock. This indicates that their vital systems—specifically the brain, heart, or lungs—are struggling to function.

Urgency Level: High (Critical Emergency)

This is a life-threatening medical emergency. You should not wait to see if the cat recovers on its own. Immediate stabilization is required at the nearest 24-hour emergency veterinary hospital. If you can, call the clinic while you are on your way so their medical team can be ready at the door the moment you arrive.

How a Photo or Video Helps Triage

If there is more than one person available, having someone take a five-second video of the cat's breathing or a clear photo of their gums can be incredibly helpful. Veterinarians look at gum color (pale, blue, or gray) and breathing effort to determine if the cause is related to oxygen levels, internal bleeding, or heart failure. This visual evidence allows the team to begin life-saving treatments the second you walk through the door.

faqs.question: Could my cat just be in a deep sleep?
faqs.answer: A healthy cat will always react to being touched, called, or having their paws pinched. If your cat does not react to these stimuli, it is a medical emergency, not sleep.

faqs.question: What should I do if my cat stops breathing on the way to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the cat's neck extended to keep the airway as open as possible. If you are trained in feline CPR, you may begin, but your primary focus must be getting to the animal hospital immediately.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of sudden collapse?
faqs.answer: Common causes include undiagnosed heart disease (like HCM), blood clots (saddle thrombus), severe dehydration, poisoning, or internal trauma.
